1:
The chapter opens at a milestone — Solomon has now spent twenty years in building — and the witnesses begin by unpacking what those twenty years hold. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke divides them plainly: seven years and a half went to the building of the temple and twelve and a half, or thirteen, to the raising of his own palace, a reckoning he sets beside the parallel at 1 Kings 7:1. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott counts the same span from a fixed point — the twenty years run from the fourth year of the reign, when the temple was founded, and take in the seven years of the temple, as 1 Kings 6:38 records, and the thirteen of the palace, as 1 Kings 7:1 tells; the opening verb, he notes, is all but identical with 1 Kings 9:10, only lightly abbreviated.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lift their eyes from the date to the design of the whole chapter. The building of the temple, they say, was the great work of Solomon's reign, beside which every other undertaking falls into the background, so that these are only summarily enumerated. In order the chapter gives the completion of various cities, important as strongholds and magazines for the defence of the kingdom; then the arrangement of the statute labour by which the works were carried out; then the regulation of the sacrificial service and public worship; and last the voyage to Ophir — the same undertakings, recounted in the same aphoristic way, that stand at 1 Kings 9:10-28, though the Chronicle adds and omits details of its own.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who had the fuller story from 1 Kings 9:10-24, turns the bare notice into a portrait of the king. Though Solomon was a man of great learning and knowledge, Henry observes, he spent his days not in contemplation but in action, not in his study but in his country, building cities and fortifying them — in a time of peace preparing for a time of war, which is as much a man's business as it is in summer to provide food for winter. The reformed John Gill is content to fix the phrase, reading the notice of the twenty years' end as the point from which the Chronicler resumes his account of the king's works. The witnesses do not divide: Clarke and Ellicott measure the years, Keil maps the chapter they open, and Henry reads in the industrious king a man who, having finished the house of God, did not sit down to ease but went out to labour for his people.
2:
The Chronicler's first notice of Solomon's building sets a puzzle, for it speaks of the cities 'which Huram had restored to Solomon,' whereas the older history tells of cities given the other way. The reformed John Gill states the received solution in brief: Solomon had first given these cities to Huram, but the Tyrian king, not liking them, returned them, and Solomon then built them up. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the whole difficulty. Literally, he says, the text reads 'which Huram gave'; and the parallel at 1 Kings 9:11-13 records what looks like the contrary transaction — Solomon giving Huram twenty cities in Galilee as a return for his services, cities that did not please Huram and so were nicknamed 'the land of Cabul,' that is, 'like-nought.' The Authorised Version, Ellicott notes, follows Josephus, who states that Huram restored the despised cities to Solomon, who thereupon repaired them and peopled them with Israelites.

Ellicott weighs the alternatives and rejects the easy ones. Some suppose an exchange of friendly gifts — Solomon giving twenty Israelite cities and Huram twenty Phoenician ones — but this he thinks highly improbable. The likelier account is that the Chronicler, or the source he follows, has passed over a fact that seemed to derogate from Solomon's greatness, namely the earlier surrender of the territory to the Tyrian king, and has chosen to speak of Huram's return of the cities as a gift. He then tells their after-fate: Solomon fortified them and colonised them with Israelites, for this border-land was chiefly inhabited by Gentiles — 'Galilee of the Gentiles,' in the phrase of Isaiah 9:1 — and, lying exposed to invaders, may well have been half-ruinous when ceded, which would explain Huram's disappointment in it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the delayed apodosis of the first: the object, the cities Huram had restored, is thrown forward in the aphoristic manner of the notice, and 'built' means not fortified only but completed, since according to 1 Kings 9:10 and 1 Kings 9:13 they had been in bad condition; then Solomon transplanted Israelites to dwell there. Against those who call the account an effacement or a travesty of the older text, Ellicott insists it is neither: it replaces the earlier statement with another that is equally true and not incompatible with it. The witnesses close ranks — Gill and Josephus supplying the harmony, Ellicott and Keil securing the sense — so that the two records are made to tell one story of ruined border-towns rebuilt and resettled by the king. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, for his part, simply refers the reader to his note on the same transaction at 1 Kings 9:11.
3:
'And Solomon went to Hamath-zobah, and prevailed against it' — and the witnesses pause over what is, in Solomon's peaceful reign, a startling sentence. The reformed John Gill marks it as the single instance of any warlike expedition of Solomon's: he went up in hostile fashion against Hamath-zobah, which Gill takes to be Coele-Syria, subdued in David's days but now perhaps rebelled, so that Solomon marched to reduce it again. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that this important statement — that Solomon went to Hamath-zobah and prevailed against it — is omitted in 1 Kings 9 altogether, and that the idiom 'to be strong upon' means to prevail against, to conquer, as at 2 Chronicles 27:5.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings the geography and a caution. 'Solomon went,' he renders, is 'marched,' the word of 2 Samuel 12:29; and Hamath-zobah means Hamath bordering on Zobah, the region of 1 Chronicles 18:3. This conquest of the kingdom of Hamath — which had been on terms of friendship with David — is mentioned neither in 1 Kings 9 nor anywhere else in the Old Testament. Ellicott follows Thenius in taking the text to describe not the reduction of Hamath itself but only the annexation of part of its territory, namely a portion of the highly fruitful plain of Coele-Syria that the Arabs call the Ard-el-Beqaa; this, he judges, appears to be correct. The phrase 'against it' he reads rather 'over it,' a late construction like those at 2 Chronicles 27:5 and Daniel 11:5.

So the witnesses agree in substance while parting mildly over scale. Whether Solomon crushed a rebel Coele-Syria, as Gill supposes, or merely annexed the rich plain that lay between his kingdom and Zobah-Damascus, as Ellicott and Thenius prefer, the point of the notice is the same: the king reached northward to the marches of Hamath and made them secure. Keil ties the whole to what follows, for it was to hold such newly-won ground that Solomon raised the strongholds and magazine-cities the next verses will name, so that this one campaign of an otherwise unwarlike reign becomes the ground of the fortress-building that fills the chapter's opening. The peaceful king, the witnesses observe, did not neglect the defence of what peace had given him; the sword that he drew but once was drawn to guard a frontier, and the towns he built in Hamath were the fruit of the ground that here he won.
4:
'And he built Tadmor in the wilderness, and all the store cities, which he built in Hamath.' The witnesses gather most eagerly around Tadmor. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke names it at once: Tadmor is Palmyra, that superb city of the desert, and he sends the reader to his fuller account at 1 Kings 9:18. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms and locates it — Palmyra, in the wilderness, on the traders' route between the coast and Thapsacus on the Euphrates; the Tamar or Tammor of the Hebrew text at 1 Kings 9:18 is explained by the margin to mean Tadmor, and the very epithet 'in the wilderness' seems to identify the two names. That Solomon founded Palmyra, Ellicott adds, is the tradition of the country to this day.

The store cities draw the same witness to their strategic worth. 'All the store cities which he built in Hamath' are mentioned at 1 Kings 9:19, Ellicott observes, but without their locality; they were no doubt places of arms, outposts against the hostile neighbouring kingdom of Zobah-Damascus, whose enmity 1 Kings 11:23-25 records, and so far as they lay on the caravan route they would serve also as victualling stations, like the store-cities of 2 Chronicles 32:28. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the same buildings in their frame: Tadmor in the wilderness and the store cities of Hamath belong to that ring of strongholds and magazines by which Solomon secured his enlarged dominion against attack. The reformed John Gill notes only, and briefly, the building of Tadmor in the wilderness, letting the fuller topography rest with the others.

The witnesses do not divide. What they build together is a picture of a kingdom reaching far into the desert north: a great caravan-city planted at an oasis on the road to the Euphrates, and a chain of fortified depots strung through the newly-secured plain of Hamath, at once guarding the frontier against Damascus and provisioning the trade that passed along it. The single campaign of the previous verse thus bears its fruit in stone; the ground Solomon had won he now held and used, so that his reach toward the Euphrates was fixed not by conquest only but by the patient work of the builder. Palmyra, the witnesses remind us, would outlast its founder by a thousand years, a monument in the sand to the king who set his outposts where no king before him had built.
5:
'Also he built Beth-horon the upper, and Beth-horon the nether, fenced cities, with walls, gates, and bars.' The witnesses fasten on what the Chronicler adds to the older record. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the two texts side by side: where 1 Kings 9:17 says only 'and Solomon built Gezer, and Beth-horon the nether,' the Chronicle names both the upper and the nether town and then describes how they were built — as fenced cities, with walls, doors, and bar, in the language of Micah 7:12 and Deuteronomy 3:5. This description of the fortifications, Ellicott points out, is wanting in Kings; 'built,' here as often, means fortified, as the rest of the verse explains, a sense he grounds in 1 Chronicles 7:24.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gather the two Beth-horons into the same class as the other strongholds: fenced cities with walls, gates, and bars, raised to defend the kingdom against attack. Beside them they set the other fortress-towns of 1 Kings 9:15-17 — Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer — which the Chronicler here passes over, even as Kings for its part omits the upper Beth-horon. The reformed John Gill remarks only that of the two towns it is the nether that is named elsewhere, the upper being brought in here to complete the pairing.

Small as the verse is, the witnesses make it tell. The Chronicler's care to record the walls, gates, and bars — the very furniture of a fortress that the older history left unmentioned — belongs to his larger interest in the strength and good order of Solomon's kingdom; he will not have the reader think the fortifying of these towns a slight thing. Upper and nether Beth-horon, guarding the road that climbed from the lowland toward the heart of the land, were made into true fenced cities, doubly barred; and their pairing with the other strongholds the witnesses cite shows a king ringing his dominion with defences at every exposed approach. The doors and their bar, named where the older text is silent, mark the completeness of the defence: not gates only, but the great cross-bar that made them fast, so that an enemy climbing the pass would find every town along it shut and barred against him. What Ellicott secures by comparing the texts, Keil reads as of a piece with the whole enterprise — the patient walling-up of a peaceful kingdom against the day of war; and in setting down even the bar upon the gate the Chronicler shows the same eye for the apparatus of strength that he elsewhere brings to the very furniture of the temple, so that the stones of Beth-horon testify to a wisdom that provided in quiet times for troubles not yet come.
6:
'And Baalath, and all the store cities that Solomon had, and all the chariot cities, and the cities of the horsemen, and all that Solomon desired to build.' The witnesses complete the roll of the king's building and mark what the Chronicler has done with it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies Baalath from 1 Kings 9:18 as a town lying, like the two Beth-horons, west of Jerusalem and serving as a bulwark against the Philistines — the Danite town of Joshua 19:44. The rest of the verse, he observes, is identical with 1 Kings 9:19, save that the Chronicler has twice added the word 'all,' heightening the sense of a building programme that spread over the whole land. And he notes what is missing here: no mention of the fortification of Jerusalem, nor of the building of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer — the last of which Pharaoh had taken and given to his daughter, Solomon's wife, as 1 Kings 9:15-16 tells; yet in return the Chronicler supplies several details the parallel lacks.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the list to its close — the store cities, the chariot cities, the cities of the horsemen, and all that Solomon desired to build in Jerusalem, in Lebanon, and throughout his whole dominion — and then turn, in the same breath, to the labour that raised them: the remnant of the Canaanite peoples, the Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites who were not of Israel, on whom the statute labour was laid, as at 1 Kings 9:20-23. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke both pass lightly here, Gill noting that from this point the account runs the same as the older record, and Clarke referring the reader for the store cities to his note at 1 Kings 9:19.

The witnesses converge on the shape of the thing. The verse gathers up the whole sweep of Solomon's works — frontier fortresses, magazine-towns, cities for the chariots and the cavalry, and all that Solomon desired to build, in the capital, in the cedar-country of Lebanon, and across every province — the twice-repeated 'all' setting the seal on a reign that built as freely as it wished. Matthew Henry had drawn the lesson from this very abundance: one project draws on another, yet Solomon knew how to set bounds to his desires, and finishing all he had a mind to build, he then desired no more — unlike those who enlarge their wants endlessly and are never satisfied. It is on the note of a king content in the completion of his work that the building-record ends and the account of his labourers begins.
8:
'But of their children, who were left after them in the land, whom the children of Israel consumed not, them did Solomon make to pay tribute unto this day.' The verse turns from the cities to the hands that built them, and here the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch are the one witness who works the ground closely. The people meant, they explain, are the remnant of the Canaanite nations named just before — the Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites who were not of Israel; and the Hebrew 'of their children' is not partitive, as though only some were taken, but points to those of their descendants who had remained in the land, whom the Israelites had never exterminated. On these, and on these alone, Solomon laid the statute labour, making them bond-servants for his works, a levy that continued, the writer notes, unto his own day.

Keil defends the reading against the parallel. The preposition 'of' is wanting in 1 Kings 9:20-21, they grant, but it is not on that account to be struck out here; the Chronicler's phrase stands, describing the surviving Canaanite population made tributary. The arrangement itself, they add, is the same laid down in the older history, and the whole notice belongs closely with the muster of the workmen already given at 2 Chronicles 2:17, where the same distinction between the enslaved stranger and the free Israelite is first drawn.

The single witness leaves the verse with its plain and sober sense. The glory of Solomon's building, the earlier verses have shown, rested on a great forced levy; but that levy fell upon the conquered peoples of the land, not upon the covenant nation. The Canaanite remnant, spared in the old wars of Joshua 9 and never rooted out, was now bound to the king's works and taxed with bond-service that outlasted Solomon himself and reached down to the Chronicler's own time. It is a hard notice, and Keil does not soften it; he only fixes exactly whom it touched — the sons of the dispossessed, still dwelling among Israel, still bearing the burden their fathers' defeat had laid on them — and so prepares the sharp contrast the next verse will draw between the bondman and the free. That closing phrase, moreover, reaches past Solomon's own reign to the age in which the Chronicler wrote, so that a labour-levy first raised for the temple and the fortress-cities was still, generations later, the settled condition of the remnant of the nations among Israel. The magnificence overhead is real; but Keil quietly reminds the reader on whose shoulders it was raised, and how long the yoke endured.
9:
'But of the children of Israel did Solomon make no servants for his work; but they were men of war, and chief of his captains, and captains of his chariots and horsemen.' Over against the Canaanite levy of the previous verse stands the freedom of Israel, and the witnesses read the contrast as deliberate. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the point and refers the reader to the fuller treatment at 1 Kings 9:21: of the covenant people Solomon made no bondmen; the servile labour was not theirs.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the honours that fell to Israel instead. The children of Israel were the men of war and the officers of the kingdom; Keil would read the words, with 1 Kings 9:22, as 'his princes and his adjutants,' for the term the Authorised Version renders among the captains means not chariot-combatants but royal adjutants, the king's own aides, in the sense the word carries at Exodus 14:7 and 2 Samuel 23:8. So the free-born filled the ranks of the army and the commands of the chariotry and cavalry, while the conquered peoples bore the burdens. The very titles the verse heaps up — men of war, chief of the captains, captains of his chariots and of his horsemen — mount a ladder of command on which only free Israelites stood, so that the whole officer-corps, from the foot-soldier to the marshal of the chariots, was drawn from the covenant people alone.

The witnesses do not divide, and the theology of the arrangement lies open in it. Israel, redeemed from the house of bondage, was not to be enslaved again, even to her own king; the yoke of forced labour was reserved for the remnant of the nations, and the sons of the covenant were given the freeman's part — to bear arms, to lead, to command. Clarke's cross-reference and Keil's grammar meet in the same picture: a kingdom whose splendour was raised by the levy of strangers, but whose defence and government were entrusted to free Israelites, so that the very roster of Solomon's servants preserved the ancient distinction between the people God had brought out of Egypt and the peoples whose land they had inherited. The verse, brief as it is, quietly guards the dignity of the chosen nation in the midst of the king's magnificence; and it prepares the reader to understand who the two hundred and fifty officers of the next verse were, and over whom they bore rule. The honour of Israel, in Solomon's Israel, was to serve as soldiers and commanders, and never as slaves.
10:
'And these were the chief of king Solomon's officers, even two hundred and fifty, that bare rule over the people.' The single witness here, the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, gives the verse its careful due, for the number has long been felt to jar with the parallel. Over the statute labourers, they explain, two hundred and fifty upper overseers were set — the 'chief of the superiors,' the head-officers who directed the work — and these bore rule over the people, that is, over the remnant of the Canaanites who made up the levy of the earlier verses.

The apparent discrepancy Keil resolves by a distinction. The older history at 1 Kings 9:23 reckons the overseers at five hundred and fifty, where the Chronicle here counts only two hundred and fifty; but the numbers do not contradict. The two hundred and fifty of the Chronicle, they argue, were the Israelite overseers, while the five hundred and fifty of Kings takes in both the Israelite and the Canaanite head-men together — a reconciliation Keil grounds in his note at 2 Chronicles 2:17, where the muster of the workmen is first given. On the disputed word itself they prefer the written text, the 'praefecti,' the officers set in charge, comparing the like usage at 1 Chronicles 18:13 and 2 Chronicles 17:2, and take the marginal reading to have crept in from 1 Kings 9:23.

The witness leaves the verse as a small window on the ordering of the whole enterprise. Beneath the king stood these two hundred and fifty Israelite chiefs, and beneath them the great mass of Canaanite labourers; the same line that ran through the two preceding verses runs through this one, dividing those who ruled from those who bore the load. Keil's patient sorting of the numbers is not idle arithmetic but the recovery of that order — free Israel set to command, the conquered remnant set to toil — so that even the tally of overseers testifies to the arrangement the Chronicler is at pains to preserve. What in the older record was a single fused figure, the Chronicle here resolves into its Israelite part; and Keil restores the sense that binds the two accounts into one, showing them not at war over a number but describing, from two angles, the one great hierarchy of Solomon's works. The Chronicler, writing for a people newly returned from exile, is careful to distinguish the native overseers from the foreign, and so counts only the two hundred and fifty who were of Israel, where the older historian, with a different purpose, had summed the Israelite and Canaanite chiefs together — a difference of reckoning, not of fact, that Keil's patient reading brings to light.
11:
'And Solomon brought up the daughter of Pharaoh out of the city of David unto the house that he had built for her.' The removal of the Egyptian queen draws the fullest cluster of witnesses in this stretch of the chapter, and they read it as a real, if imperfect, act of reverence. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the reason Solomon assigns — that the places are holy, whereunto the ark of the LORD had come — and asks pointedly whether this is not proof that the king considered his wife a heathen, unfit to dwell where holiness had been; yet, Clarke adds, Solomon had not yet departed from the true God, so that the scruple, however it judged the queen, still sprang from a living regard for the sacred. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the motive the same way: Solomon built this house for her because the ark was now in the house of David, which therefore ought to be kept pure and free from the very danger and appearance of pollution.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the removal into a lesson on the ancient landmarks. Solomon would not suffer the place where the ark of God had been, and where holy David had prayed many a good prayer and sung many a sweet psalm, to be profaned by the gaiety — not to say the iniquity — that may have filled the court of Pharaoh's daughter; and though he had not zeal enough to suppress what was amiss among her people, he at least consulted the honour of his father's memory, keeping up between things sacred and things common the landmarks that ought never to be removed.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fix the history and the language. Charles Ellicott notes that the Chronicler, who has nowhere else mentioned this princess, brings her in here only in connection with Solomon's buildings, and that the motive 'for he said' is wanting in the older, briefer notice at 1 Kings 9:24 — the reason being characteristic of the Chronicler in thought and language, and 'king of Israel' set pointedly against the queen's Egyptian origin; he thinks it too much to say, with Thenius, that she could have dwelt nowhere but the old palace of David until the new one was built. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the removal is introduced abruptly, as at 1 Kings 9:24, because it belongs to the history of the king's buildings, and read the ground of it plainly: Solomon could not allow an Egyptian to dwell in the palace of King David, which the presence of the ark had hallowed. The witnesses do not divide. Whether they hear in the verse, with Clarke, an unconscious verdict on the heathen wife, or with Henry a guarding of holy ground, or with Ellicott the Chronicler's own devout hand, they agree that Solomon's care for the sanctity of David's house was genuine — the last, perhaps, of his undivided devotion before the strange wives of his later years drew his heart away.
12:
'Then Solomon offered burnt offerings unto the LORD on the altar of the LORD, which he had built before the porch.' With the buildings done, the witnesses follow the king from his works to his worship. The reformed John Gill guards against a narrow reading of 'then': Solomon did not sacrifice only at the time he had rebuilt the cities, but this was his constant practice, the settled devotion of the reign. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott says the same of the tense — 'offered' means not once but habitually, according to the prescriptions of the Mosaic law that the next verse will unfold — and marks two things the Chronicler notes that 1 Kings 9:25 does not: that the offerings were laid on the altar Solomon himself had built, and that this altar stood 'before the porch' of the temple.

That last point the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press home. The brief notice of 1 Kings 9:25, that Solomon sacrificed three times a year, is here expanded into a full account; and its force is that the king now offered upon the great altar before the porch of the new house, and no longer upon the altar of the old tabernacle at Gibeon, where he had sacrificed at the beginning of his reign in 2 Chronicles 1:3. The divided worship of the early chapters — ark at Jerusalem, altar at Gibeon — is healed at last; the temple gathers both under one roof, and the king's sacrifice moves to the place God had chosen.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the devotion out. The building of the temple, he says, was in order to the service of the temple; whatever cost Solomon had been at in rearing the structure, had he neglected the worship to be performed there it would all have been to no purpose — and assisting the devotion of others will never atone for our own neglects. In vain had the altar been built, and in vain had fire come down from heaven, if sacrifices had not been constantly brought as the food of the altar and the fuel of that fire. So the witnesses close ranks around a single truth: the great work of the reign was not the house but the worship it existed to shelter, and Solomon, having finished the one, gave himself at once and habitually to the other, at the altar he had raised before the porch of the LORD. The devotion that had begun the reign at Gibeon now comes home to Zion, and the king who built the sanctuary is the first to fill it with sacrifice.
13:
'Even after a certain rate every day, offering according to the commandment of Moses, on the sabbaths, and on the new moons, and on the solemn feasts, three times in the year.' The verse lays out the ordered cycle of Israel's worship, and the witnesses attend both to its rhythm and to its words. The reformed John Gill fixes the ground-note — the daily sacrifice, morning and evening, appointed in the law. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening more exactly as 'with a day's matter on a day,' the idiom of Leviticus 23:37, and notes a small mark of the Chronicler's later Hebrew — the form he uses for 'solemn feasts,' the set seasons whose three names follow, occurs in this shape here only.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse the sentence with care. The clause about the daily rate, they say, is a supplementary, explanatory aside interpolated into the main statement, which runs on: Solomon offered sacrifices on the sabbaths, the new moons, and the appointed feasts; and 'the daily rate' means precisely what the law of Moses prescribed for each day, as at Leviticus 23:37. The three chief feasts the verse then names — the feast of unleavened bread, the feast of weeks, and the feast of tabernacles — the pilgrim festivals at which all Israel came up before the LORD.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the whole into a pattern of piety. There were daily sacrifices, he observes, a certain rate every day as duly as the day came; weekly sacrifices on the sabbath, double what was offered on other days; monthly sacrifices at the new moons; and yearly sacrifices at the three solemn feasts. Those are spiritual sacrifices, Henry adds, that are now required of us, which we are to bring daily and weekly — and it is good to be in a settled method of devotion. The witnesses meet without a jar: Gill and Keil and Ellicott secure the letter and the law behind the calendar, while Henry reads in its steady round the very shape of a devout life, the offering of each day in its day, that the reader is invited to make his own. What the Chronicler records of Solomon's temple, they agree, is no cold routine but the appointed and constant rendering of Israel's worship, kept exactly as Moses had commanded, from the morning lamb to the great feasts of the turning year.
14:
'And he appointed, according to the order of David his father, the courses of the priests to their service, and the Levites to their charges.' The witnesses read the verse as Solomon's fidelity to the pattern his father had set. The reformed John Gill names the arrangement: the four-and-twenty courses of the priests, which served in their weekly turns, ordered as David had appointed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders 'he appointed' as 'caused to stand,' set in place, and glosses the several offices — the courses of the priests of 1 Chronicles 24, the 'charges' or watches of the Levites, their appointed task 'to praise' as in 1 Chronicles 25:3, their ministry before the priests as in 1 Chronicles 23:28, and the porters by their courses at every gate, after the order of 1 Chronicles 26:1-19.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the whole: Solomon ordered the temple service entirely according to the arrangement David had introduced, setting the classes of the priests to their service and the Levites to their stations, to praise and to serve before the priests as the duty of each day required, and the doorkeepers to their courses — the same standing ordinance echoed at Nehemiah 12:24. Every rank had its appointed place — priest, singing Levite, minister, and gatekeeper — and each was set to its work as the duty of each day required, so that nothing in the daily round of the house was left to chance or to the king's own devising. What the witnesses stress is that Solomon added nothing and altered nothing; the wise king bowed to the order the man of God had given.

For David is here called 'the man of God,' and the title draws its own comment. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley explains it: David is so named as a prophet, inspired by God in these very matters, so that his commands concerning the temple service are the commands of God, and not merely the arrangements of a king. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry says the same and honours Solomon for heeding it — though a wise and great man and the builder of the temple, he did not attempt to amend, alter, or add to what the man of God had, in God's name, commanded, but closely adhered to it and used his royal authority to see it duly observed. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a king whose devotion lay not in innovation but in obedience, keeping the courses and charges his father had received from God, so that the worship of the new house ran in the ancient channels the Spirit had cut through David.
15:
'And they departed not from the commandment of the king unto the priests and Levites concerning any matter, or concerning the treasures.' The verse records the faithful keeping of the order just described, and the witnesses divide, gently, over whose commandment is meant. The reformed John Gill reads it of Solomon's: the priests and Levites departed not from the commandment of the king, which followed the order of David, but in all things obeyed it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, on the other hand, takes 'the commandment of the king' to mean the institutions of David — the king whose ordinances these were from the first.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott settles the question with Clarke and adds a textual note. 'The king,' he says, is David, whose arrangements in 1 Chronicles 24-26 the verse assures us were faithfully observed by his successor; and he restores a word that has dropped from the Hebrew — the little preposition 'from,' preserved in three manuscripts and the versions — so that the sense is that they turned not aside from David's command in any matter, and especially not concerning the treasures, the temple stores whose keeping is ordered at 1 Chronicles 26:20-28. Keil and Delitzsch read the grammar the same way, taking the verb 'to depart' with its object as meaning to transgress a command, and referring 'concerning the priests and Levites' to what David had laid upon them.

Beneath the small divergence the witnesses are one. Whether the commandment be named as Solomon's, enforcing David's order, or as David's directly, the obedience is the same: the whole service, down to the guarding of the treasures, was carried on exactly as it had been appointed, without the least departure. Ellicott draws the plain conclusion — that these two verses assure us the elaborate arrangements of David were kept without change by the son who inherited them; and so the Chronicler, having shown Solomon establishing the worship, now shows priests and Levites maintaining it, the king's authority and the ministers' fidelity meeting to keep the house of God in the order its founder had received from heaven. The special mention of the treasures is not incidental: the temple stores, the dedicated things and the tithes that David had committed to the Levites' keeping, were exactly the kind of trust most open to abuse, and the note that the ministers turned aside from the order concerning the treasures least of all is a quiet testimony to their integrity in handling what was holy. It is a fitting tribute: in Solomon's day, and by the Chronicler's account, the ordinance held, and not a man turned aside from it.
16:
'Now all the work of Solomon was prepared unto the day of the foundation of the house of the LORD, and until it was finished. So the house of the LORD was perfected.' The witnesses read this as the seal set on the whole account of the temple. The reformed John Gill takes 'prepared' of the materials — the money, the stones, the timber, all made ready and fitted, so that nothing was left to retard the completion of the work. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it the same way: all the materials were procured, and in all points fitted and completed beforehand.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the words more finely. 'Prepared,' he says, here means completed — a late use of the term, found again at 2 Chronicles 29:35 and 2 Chronicles 35:16; and the phrase 'unto the day of the foundation... until it was finished' seems to divide Solomon's activity into two periods — the preparations made up to the founding of the temple, and the prosecution of the work to its completion. The ancient versions, however, read 'from the day of the foundation unto the completion,' and Ellicott allows that the Chronicler's idiom may indeed mean 'from that day,' marking the point from which the reckoning runs. The closing clause he ties to 1 Kings 9:25, where the same root stands: 'he finished the house.'

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the moment the verse celebrates. 'The day' is the day on which, the temple being dedicated, the regular public worship according to the law of Moses was begun in it — for only when that ordered service was set up was the house truly complete. And here the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays his finger on the heart of the matter: when the service of the temple was put into this good order, then, and not before, it is said that the house of the LORD was perfected — the work was the main matter, not the place; the temple was unfinished till all this was done. The witnesses meet in one confession. The building of walls and the casting of vessels had not perfected the house; it was perfected only when the worship for which it was built began to rise within it, so that the Chronicler's 'finished' means not the last stone laid but the first sacrifice offered in the settled round of praise.
17:
'Then went Solomon to Ezion-geber, and to Eloth, at the sea side in the land of Edom.' The chapter turns from worship to commerce, and the witnesses take up the king's venture to the sea. The reformed John Gill reads the moment simply: Solomon, now at leisure from his building, turned to look after his navy and his merchandise; and he adds that these two verses need to be squared with the parallel record, where the fleet is called Solomon's own — a reconciliation that will lie, as the next verse shows, in the Tyrian partnership, Huram supplying both the ships and the crews for a king whose people had no seafaring of their own. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sends the reader to his fuller conjectures on Ezion-geber and Ophir at 1 Kings 9:26-28, the parallel account of the same voyage.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the time and refines the wording. 'Then,' he says, is after the completion of the temple; and the older text at 1 Kings 9:26 reads that 'a fleet did king Solomon make at Ezion-geber, which is near Eloth,' while the Syriac calls it a city over against Eloth. 'The sea' is, in Kings, the Red Sea. And Ellicott enters a caution: the words of the Chronicle do not necessarily imply that Solomon went in person to the Edomite coast; it may be that he sent his shipwrights down to the port on the gulf, and the 'going' is the sending of the work.

Here the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse the other way, and finds a lesson in the king's own presence. Solomon did himself in person visit the sea-port towns of Eloth and Ezion-geber, Henry judges; for those who deal much in the world will find it their interest, so far as they can, to inspect their affairs themselves and to see with their own eyes. So a small difference opens between the witnesses — whether the verse shows the king travelling to the coast to oversee his fleet, as Henry supposes, or only despatching his men to build it there, as Ellicott allows — but it is a difference of picture, not of substance. All agree on the thing itself: that Solomon, his temple finished, reached out through the Edomite harbours toward the trade of distant Ophir, and that the wealth God had promised him began to come in by sea. The reign that had built for God now built for gain, and the witnesses let the two stand side by side without reproach.
18:
'And Huram sent him by the hands of his servants ships, and servants that had knowledge of the sea; and they went with the servants of Solomon to Ophir, and took thence four hundred and fifty talents of gold.' The last verse tells of the Tyrian partnership that made the voyage possible, and the witnesses divide the labour of explaining it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set down the plain sense: Huram sent ships and seamen who knew the sea, and they sailed with Solomon's servants to Ophir and brought back the gold — a place, Keil holds, to be sought not in India but in southern Arabia.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains why the Tyrians were needed. 'Knowledge of the sea' means skilful sailors; Solomon bore the expenses, and his friend the king of Tyre furnished the expert mariners, for the Jews at no period of their history had any skill in maritime affairs, their navigation being confined to the lakes of their own country. The Tyrians, by contrast, lived on and in the sea. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes up the harder questions — to what port the ships were sent, and how they reached the Red Sea at all. Probably, he thinks, the Tyrian vessels put in at Joppa, as at 2 Chronicles 2:16, and a portion of their crews then proceeded overland, by Solomon's order, to Ezion-geber; the notion that whole ships were dragged across the desert or sailed round Africa he calls a dilemma only apparent, since 'ships and servants' means simply ships conveying servants.

On the gold the witnesses face a discrepancy and resolve it. The Chronicle here reads four hundred and fifty talents, where the older account at 1 Kings 9:28 reads four hundred and twenty; and Ellicott lays the difference to a scribe's error, the Hebrew letter for fifty being easily confused with that for twenty. The mariners were Huram's — 'and they went,' Ellicott notes, are the Tyrian crews of 1 Kings 9:27 — and the port of return was Ophir, the Sophira of the Greek. So the chapter ends where the promise of the first chapter pointed: the riches God had pledged to the young king at 2 Chronicles 1:12 now flowing in by the hands of a foreign ally and his practised seamen. The witnesses close on the wealth without a word of blame here, though they have shown throughout that all of it — cities, worship, and gold alike — rested on the God who had made the young king strong.
